name: Northern Neighbors
description: Distinguish between Swedish, Finnish, and Estonian

questions.explanation: Swedish is a Germanic language. Words like 'var' (where) and 'ligger' (lies/is located) are cognates with English and German.
Finnish uses long double vowels ('lähin') and specific suffixes. It is a Finno-Ugric language, distinct from Swedish.
Estonian is closely related to Finnish but often 'drops' the final vowel seen in Finnish words (peatus vs pysäkki).
Uses the characteristic Germanic 'jag' (I) and the verb 'pratar' (speak).
Finnish negation uses a conjugated verb 'en' followed by the main verb.
Estonian uses 'ma' for 'I' and 'ei' as a universal negation particle, unlike the conjugated Finnish version.
Swedish uses 'tack' for please/thank you, similar to the Norwegian and Danish 'tak'.
Finnish often ends loanwords with an 'i' (Kuppi) and uses 'kiitos' for thanks.
Estonian uses 'ü' (like German) and 'palun' for please.
The 'Välkommen' is a classic North Germanic greeting.
The 'Terve-' prefix is common in Finnish greetings.
Estonian 'Tere' is shorter than the Finnish 'Tervetuloa'.
Swedish adds '-et' to the end of nouns (Huset) to indicate 'the'.
Finnish colors often end in '-inen'.
Estonian words are often shorter versions of their Finnish cousins (punane vs punainen).
A standard Germanic question structure meaning 'What is your name?'.
Uses the possessive suffix '-si' on the word name (nimi).
Uses 'mis' for what and 'su' as a short form of your.
Features the Swedish 'idag' (today) and Germanic 'snöar' (snowing).
Finnish uses 'lunta' (partitive of lumi) to describe the snow falling.
Note the word 'täna' (today), which lacks the double vowels of Finnish 'tänään'.
Swedish 'eller' (or) and 'kött' (meat) are very common vocabulary words.
The suffix '-kö' turns the verb 'syöt' into a question.

questions.text: Kas sa sööd liha või kala?
questions.explanation: Estonian uses the particle 'Kas' at the start of a sentence to form a question.
Swedish days of the week end in '-dag'.
Finnish days are borrowed from Germanic but given a distinct vowel-heavy spelling.
Estonian days end in '-päev' (day).
The letter 'ö' at the start of a word is common in Swedish commands.
Uses 'ikkuna' for window, a loan from Russian but adapted to Finnish phonology.
The Estonian word 'aken' is related to Finnish 'ikkuna' but significantly shortened.
